data.title: Republic Bancorp: Upgrading To Hold, Earnings Likely To Grow
data.description:
Republic Bancorp's (NASDAQ:RBCAA) stock price has plunged by around 9% since I last wrote an article on the company. Further, the earnings reported for the fourth quarter missed my expectations. After updating my model for the fourth quarter's results, I'm now estimating an EPS of $5.54 for 2025, which is slightly lower than my previous estimate. I'm expecting loan growth and a lower provision expense to drive earnings this year. The year-end target price is still below the current market price, as was the case when I last wrote about the company. However, the total expected return is now no longer too bad. Therefore, I'm upgrading Republic Bancorp to a hold rating.

Stock Price Rout Since Last Report Calls for Valuation Update

I adopted a sell rating on Republic Bancorp in my last report on the company, which was published on November 7, 2024. As expected, the stock price has fallen by ~9% since the issuance of that report. During this time, Republic Bancorp has also announced its full-year results with an EPS of $5.14, which fell short of my previous estimate.

The provisions expense for loan losses surprisingly shot up during the last quarter, which was the biggest reason why earnings missed my expectations. Republic Bancorp's non-performing loans ("NPL") also rose during the quarter; so, although the company raised its allowances for loan losses, the allowances-to-NPL ratio worsened during the quarter. However, the ratio is still quite high and more than comfortable at 404%.

Another reason why the earnings missed my expectations was that the operating expenses shot up during the quarter, which led to a worse-than-expected efficiency ratio.

Provisioning Will Likely Ease in 2025

Republic Bancorp's provision expense for loan losses has shot up in the last two years. In fact, the provision expense is now higher than it was during the pandemic in 2020.

Part of the provisioning buildup is attributable to the asset quality, which has worsened of late. However, the asset quality still isn't that bad from a historical point of view. The NPL to total loan ratio was much worse in 2019 and 2020.

Looking at the loan book, it's not clear why the management has become so much more prudent over the last couple of years. Republic Bancorp adopted CECL in January 2020, so there has been no change in accounting rules over the last couple of years. Further, the company's loan book is well-diversified, with a branch network spanning metropolitan areas in five states. The loan book is well-diversified by loan segments as well. The biggest loan segment, commercial real estate, makes up 33% of total loans and the second-biggest loan segment, residential real estate, makes up 25% of total loans.

Republic Bancorp has not disclosed the balance of loans collateralized by office properties; so, it's difficult to determine the credit risk facing the company. The company does have some exposure to credit card and automobile loans, which are generally considered to be riskier than other loans, especially real estate loans. However, this exposure is limited as credit card loans made up just 0.3% of total loans, and automobile loans made up just 0.02% of total loans at the end of December.

The management mentioned in 4Q 2024's earnings release that the quarter's heightened provisions were due to loan additions in the traditional banking division (other divisions are warehouse banking and Republic Processing Group). The management did not discuss any problem loans in particular. The allowances-to-NPLs ratio stood at 404% at the end of the last quarter, which is quite high and more than comfortable as long as there are no systemic credit problems.

All in all, I think the loan portfolio's credit quality is fine, and I think the provisioning can ease in 2025, but I'm not very confident as Republic Bancorp has not made the disclosures that I need, especially the exposure to office property loans.

I'm expecting a provision expense of $48 million for 2025, which is 0.9% of total loans, down from 1.0% of loans last year. In my last report, I projected a provision expense of only $24 million for 2025, which seems quite naively optimistic now after the fourth quarter's results. The second and third quarter's results had given me hope that we could return to the pre-2023 level, but I was obviously very wrong.

Net Interest Income Outlook Remains Positive

Republic Bancorp's net interest income finished the year on a positive note. The full year's net interest income grew by an impressive 8% to $312 million, which was close to my previous estimate of $307 million.

I think the net interest income growth might slow down this year, mostly because I think loan growth in 2025 won't be able to match last year's loan growth. The loan book grew by 3.7% in 2024, which is close to the last five-year compounded annual growth rate of 4.2%. The company's loan growth has been quite volatile and extreme from year to year, having fallen by 6.7% in 2021 and risen by 16% in 2023.

As summarized below, Republic Bancorp's branch network is concentrated in Kentucky. Tampa, FL, is also a key operating area for the company.

Kentucky's unemployment rate has shot up since I last covered Republic Bancorp. Meanwhile, Tampa's unemployment rate continues to remain low.

Further, the house price indices for most of Republic Bancorp's markets have gotten steeper in 2024, with the exception of Florida. The growth of housing prices in most states is not a good signal for Republic Bancorp's residential real estate segment, as it affects housing affordability. The dip in housing prices in Florida is also not a positive signal because of the devastation caused by Hurricanes Milton and Helene.

Overall, I'm expecting the loan book to grow by 3.0% in 2025. Compared to my last report on the company, I haven't changed my loan growth estimate for this year, but my average loan balance estimate is higher now because the loan balance at the end of December 2024 was higher than I had expected.

I'm assuming deposits and borrowings will grow in line with loans.

Another factor that will affect the net interest income in 2025 will be the net interest margin. The average margin in 2024 was 6 basis points below the average margin for 2023. After the 100 basis points Fed funds rate cuts in the latter part of 2024, I'm assuming no cuts in 2025. My outlook is mostly based on the tariffs, which have created an environment of uncertainty. The management's rate simulation model showed in last September that 100 basis points rate cuts would have a negligible impact on the net interest income.

As a result, I'm expecting the average margin to remain unchanged this year. The margin will jump up in the first quarter and then normalize for the remainder of the year because of seasonality factors. Republic Bancorp's net interest margin usually spikes in the first quarter of the year.

Overall, I'm expecting the company to report a net interest income of $325 million for 2025, up 4% year-over-year. Compared to my last report on the company, I've slightly raised my net interest income estimate because my loan balance estimate is higher than before following the fourth quarter's above-expected loan growth.

Now Expecting an EPS of $5.54 for 2025

The low loan growth will likely be the biggest earnings catalyst in 2025. Further, I'm expecting the provision expense in 2025 to be lower compared to last year's provision expense, which will support earnings growth. Meanwhile, I'm assuming the non-interest income will be stable and the non-interest expense will move in line with revenues so that the efficiency ratio barely changes.

Overall, I'm estimating earnings per share of $5.54 for 2025, up 8% year-over-year. In my last report, I projected an EPS of $5.62 for this year. I've reduced my earnings estimate mostly because I've raised my provision expense estimate following the fourth quarter's results.

Upgrading to Hold

Republic Bancorp raised its quarterly dividend by 11% to $0.451 for the first quarter of 2025. The new dividend suggests a dividend yield of 2.7%. Moreover, my earnings estimates and the new dividend level suggest a payout ratio of 33%, so the dividend appears safe from any cuts.

I'm using the historical average price-to-earnings ("P/E") and price-to-tangible book ("P/TB") multiples to value Republic Bancorp.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple with my forecast for EPS gives a target price of $60.3 for December 31, 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple with my TBVPS forecast gives a target price of $63.7 for December 31, 2025.

The average target price comes out to $62.0. In my last report, I gave a target price of $62.2 for December 2025 and adopted a sell rating. My target price is a bit lower now, as my earnings estimate is also a bit lower.

My new target price suggests an 8.3% downside from the February 19 closing market price. Adding the dividend yield of 2.7% to the price downside gives a total expected return of negative 5.7%. This isn't so bad; therefore, I think it's time to upgrade Republic Bancorp to a hold rating.

The market is a perpetual paradox machine where one does not go to fulfill the need for clarity and cohesion. Instead, one will find a continuing oscillation between rationalizations for extreme moves. The cheapest quartile of stocks is cheaper now than in 2014 and the most expensive quartile is much more expensive, partly a function of capital flooding out of active and into passive (passive now ~54% of total US equity assets 1). The divergences between the valuation of similar assets can often reach proportions that are hard to fathom and can’t easily be explained by anything empirical, but the best long/short equity opportunities are born from these extreme contradictions that we find ongoing today, thus there is a superb stock picking backdrop.

The consistency of price momentum factor performance has put it in rarified air, such that many prominent parts of The Great Humiliator are breaching the confines of historical probability and trampling on the Gaussian curve. Rolling 9-month price momentum of large cap stocks has only done better on four occasions in market history, with the end of 2020/start of 2021 being one of those rare time periods. 2

Retail investor inflows and flows across nearly all asset classes have shattered records so far in 2025 3, underscoring a persistent imbalance—there is too much capital chasing too few assets combined with limited new asset creation. In this environment, a new breed of nihilistic traders has spawned over 40 million intrinsically worthless cryptocurrencies, all while deriding rigorous fundamental analysis and detailed valuation.

The S&P 500 was up 27% in 2024 with just 8% EPS growth expected for the year—the P/E multiple expanded by 15% even with the 10-year yield up >70 bps. Q4 2024 earnings have been solid so far and shown an acceleration of growth, with every sector beating consensus expectations with total year-over-year growth clocking in at a robust 16.7% (7% above expectations) 4. At a pricey 23x 2025 earnings with 14% EPS growth already embedded in that forward P/E ratio (the trailing 20+ year average EPS growth has been closer to 7.2%), we will see if historically “normal” valuations are given a peaceful reentrance onto Wall Street ushered in by continued boomy EPS growth the next several years or if valuations will violently plop down under the weight of unrealistic earnings growth expectations. In other words, sustained earnings growth and a strong ROIC in the tech sector will be crucial to keeping overall market multiples above 20x, with the market’s perception of burgeoning AI-related capex playing a key role.

Small caps keep trying to hold alpha’s face, but it quickly turns away time and time again. Regardless of how factor or style leadership shakes out this year, our investment team’s main theme and focus coming into 2025 has been on small cap M&A and special situations as we felt there has been pent up demand for deals after two years of dismal deal flow. This emphasis on M&A is paying early dividends with three quick wins from acquisitions in our long book year-to-date: SWI, CTS CN, PLYA.

SolarWinds (SWI) makes for an interesting case study of low expectations, as we had rarely witnessed such apathy across both the buyside and sell side, along with a strange reluctance to move on from the past cybersecurity breach in 2020 despite clear and ongoing operational execution for several years in a row. We wrote in our Q1 2024 letter that “we think the odds are elevated that the company will receive a buyout offer from a different PE fund within the next year or so.” Given the fact pattern, it was a large position for the fund and Voss was the largest active outside shareholder when the buyout was announced. SWI was undergoing a perpetual license to subscription transition with subscription ARR growth of 36% in the most recent quarter, with total ARR growth having accelerated up to 8.4% from low single digits several quarters ago, and total revenue growth improving from 0% to 5%. Where the market saw a sleepy low/no growth legacy software company, we saw a clear deleveraging story with improving net revenue retention, an ongoing acceleration of total ARR growth (driven by an efficient sales force) occurring since 2022, and a 52%+ on the software “Rule of 40” scale trading for under 9x FCF. The frequent subject of buyout rumors the last few years, SWI finally found a new home in Turn/River Capital at 5.2x EV/Revenue and 13.8x our 2025 FCF estimates, a little below our base case valuation, but a win/win for all stakeholders.

Converge Technology Solutions Group (CTS CN)(OTCQX:CTSDF), a technology services company and distributor in the vein of CDW, fit into the same depressed sentiment, low valuation penalty box as SWI. Voss first became interested in the company as they were going through a strategic review a little over a year ago while trading at around 5x FCF. Although that review did not result in a buyout, we began adding to our position as we witnessed the newly appointed CFO (with glowing reviews from his previous stint at Dye & Durham (DYNDF)) unlock an enormous amount of free cash flow (~$275 million on a market cap of ~$700 million) and de-lever the company to well below one times EBITDA, all while maintaining positive revenue growth and beating EBITDA expectations. Although the company ended that streak in Q3 with a miss and guided down, we witnessed similarly challenged numbers across the trading comp set in names like CDW and ePlus and determined that it was far more of a transient macro issue than a company-specific issue. Perhaps there was a bit of global valuation arbitrage at work, but the US names shrugged off the macro headwinds (CDW moved from 13x to 15x NTM EBITDA) while Converge sunk back to the sub-5x EBITDA levels they endured as a far more levered company. Although our thesis was the company would sell off their underperforming, subscale Europe business and aggressively buy back stock, it is our understanding that multiple parties approached Converge and they had three offers all around the takeout price of $5.50 CAD (~56% premium) and settled on H.I.G. Capital.

Playa Hotels & Resorts (PLYA) was one of our “easier” and more straight-forward investments that we wrote about publicly. An owner and operator of all-inclusive resorts in Mexico, Jamaica, and the Dominican Republic, we long thought that PLYA traded below both private market valuations and replacement cost. Given that it was executing well and still not getting credit in public markets, we believed the best course of action was to hang up the for-sale sign. When the company made out-of-cycle changes to their executive change-in-control payouts in September 2024, increasing the payouts to top executives in a sale and doing so three months before their existing employment agreements were set to expire, we significantly upsized the position (buying over 6% of the company), even calling out Hyatt as the most likely acquirer. Hyatt announced they were acquiring PLYA for $13.50 per share in early February. While the ultimate outcome and sale price was slightly disappointing (8% below the bottom of our estimated value range), our incremental purchases had a great IRR, as the stock has risen >75% from the time of the change-in-control amendments just five short months ago.

Good things should keep happening to value stocks like SWI, CTS CN and PLYA, and we expect several additional portfolio companies to be acquired as the year progresses.

Portfolio Position Updates

Long Euronet (EEFT)

EEFT’s stock had been whacked down to an all-time low valuation coming into its Q4 earnings report, presumably on the fears of the new Trump administration’s immigration policies (despite far fewer immigrants being deported under Trump than Obama on an annual basis) affecting Euronet’s money transfer business, Ria, along with general apathy in the payments space. We think these fears have been drastically overblown and it is overall economic growth (and construction employment for the US to Mexico corridor) that is far more important to its business than moderate immigrant deportations (let alone the fact that Ria is an international business and the US to Latin America corridor is a small piece of that business). At current valuation levels of just ~5.4x 2026 EBITDA and 9x 2026 earnings despite remarkably consistent execution and a forecasted reacceleration of growth, we believe EEFT remains undervalued with significant re-rating potential over the duration of 2025.

A part of our Euronet thesis is around ATM take rates. While most “FinTech” companies are constantly concerned about take rate compression, Euronet seems to have taken rate tailwinds in their most known and maligned segment, ATMs (which falls within their Electronic Funds Transfer, or EFT, segment). A little over a year ago the company settled an antitrust lawsuit with Visa and Mastercard, who they believed had colluded with each other to put up roadblocks to increased fees on ATMs. Management commented recently that while that settlement was a first step, it was the countries themselves that needed to formally clear the way in allowing more surcharges on ATMs, something they stated is now happening. We believe a significant bump in charges (which come in at 100% pretax margins) after a decade of compression with rising costs could give the company significantly more confidence in growth from their ATMs and could change the narrative around the ATM business as a secular decliner. The last couple quarters have shown the EFT segment generating > 50% rolling incremental EBITDA margins, something we believe is reflective of early improvements in EEFT’s ATM take rate.

Beyond that, the rest of the business is growing fast enough that ATMs are slowly becoming less relevant. Long a driver of the questionable investment narrative of EEFT being a dying cash-based business, the ATM segment now represents only 19% of revenue (and 35% of EBITDA, down from >60% in 2019), with secular growth businesses like their acquired Merchant Services business (from Piraeus Bank in Greece), REN (real-time payments software), and Dandelion (B2B cross border payments technology) increasing as contributors, and Ria aggressively moving from “brick-and-mortar” to “digital” money remittances. We look forward to increasing disclosures around those businesses’ contributions throughout 2025. To summarize, the company’s most maligned business has substantial, narrative altering tailwinds this year, and their growth businesses seem to be building enough momentum to change the narrative (and valuation multiples) on the company from a “secular decliner” to more of a leading edge fintech company.

We believe management’s recent guidance reflects this growing optimism, even if they are unwilling to spell out some of the potential regulatory benefits. Historically conservative in nature, management guided for accelerating EPS growth in 2025, moving their “10-15% EPS growth” framework to “12-16%”, even after noting that 2025 would be an unusually heavy tax year (high 20s vs ~24% historically). This effectively changed the midpoint of their underlying EPS guide from 12.5% growth to over 16%, which by our math implies at least 14-15% EBITDA growth and 10% revenue growth. Given this profile, we value Euronet with a Base Case EV/EBITDA multiple of 9x, and a conservative 13x 2026 EPS for a company projected to grow EPS between 13-20%. Euronet’s growth re-acceleration across revenue, EBITDA, and operating income, along with strong guidance, signals a return to strong operational momentum such that a “Bull Case” scenario is also becoming increasingly likely. In this case, Euronet gets back closer to its historical multiples (15-20x NTM earnings) with improved disclosures on higher-growth segments and waning risk around their lower growth segments. This could push the stock closer towards the low end of its historical multiple of 15x P/E ($170 PT), with potential upside to 20x NTM earnings if REN and Dandelion gain traction (116% upside).

Long Mercedes-Benz Group (OTCPK:MBGYY)

Mercedes-Benz is a luxury focused global automotive manufacturer with resilient free cash flow generation that is being undervalued by the market. The company has actively positioned itself toward high margin luxury vehicles at the top end of the market, providing a buffer from the price competitive mass-market segment, particularly from aggressive Chinese original equipment manufacturers (OEMs). Of note, Mercedes is the first carmaker to have Level 3 (the manufacturer assumes full legal risk) autonomous vehicles on the road in the US (aside from Waymo) and we think they will continue to be technology leaders in the autonomous vehicle space. While concerns about Mercedes’ slowing growth in China and structural inefficiencies in European production persist, an inflection in MBGYY’s capital return policies is what got us interested in the stock recently. In mid-2024, Mercedes committed to returning 100% of industrial FCF to shareholders, with a 40% payout ratio for dividends (an expected ~8% yield at current levels), with the remaining 60% going to share repurchases (set to buy back ~7% of shares out annually at current levels).

At ~6x Price/2025E earnings (and <2x EV/2025E earnings) along with a ~15% total shareholder return, we find that MBG is a compelling value stock with limited downside risk. If the company can further optimize their balance sheet, there is significant re-rating potential. In a base case scenario valuing the company at 6.8x industrial FCF and giving them credit for excess cash and non-operating investments (equal to ~€42B when including its cash balance, F1 stake at a 25% discount to Forbes’ estimated value, and €10.8B for their Daimler Truck stake), the stock has ~66% upside.

Long Subaru (OTCPK:FUJHY)

Subaru is a consistently profitable Japanese auto OEM with most of its sales in the US, not to mention it has the most entertaining auto memes on social media. The company has been FCF positive every year since 2009. With incessant tariff headlines, most auto OEMs other than Tesla (TSLA) (and perhaps Rivian and Ferrari) are extremely out of favor with many valued at ~1x EBITDA (compare this to negative growth, lower margin TSLA at ~100x). Subaru has been further punished due to being slow to develop electric vehicles. We think this has proven a prudent strategy as EV's are now rapidly bleeding market share globally and OEMs are having to heavily discount already unprofitable vehicles to sell them, meanwhile Subaru is sitting pretty from an inventory perspective. Subaru is 21% owned by Toyota (TM)and is engaged in joint development programs for plug-in hybrid vehicles and electric vehicles with the largest automaker in the world to realize more efficiency with shared platforms and resources. Should Toyota continue to try and clean up its capital structure and balance sheet by eliminating crossholdings, Subaru would make for a great bite-sized acquisition (note Toyota originally paid ~7x EBITDA for its Subaru stake versus current valuation at ~1x). Like Mercedes-Benz Group, Subaru recently initiated a share buyback and more aggressive capital return policy, without which we admit that the shares could be more likely to morph into a value trap without a catalyst. The company has committed to returning at least 40% of earnings each year, and it clearly has the capacity to distribute well above that amount. With a healthy >$10B net cash position and an enterprise value approaching zero despite consistent free cash flow generation, it is safe to say that market’s forward expectations for cash flow are quite low for Subaru, and we are getting the operating business almost for free. At just 6x CY2026E earnings plus the $10b net cash on the balance sheet, we think FUJHY has ~60% upside.

Long Amentum (AMTM)

Amentum (AMTM) is a newly formed, under-covered government services leader trading at an unwarranted 30%+ discount to trading peers due to spin-off dynamics and near-term selling pressure. With a $45 billion backlog (3.2x annual revenue), strong cash generation and a clear deleveraging path, AMTM offers a compelling re-rate opportunity as it executes its post- merger growth strategy.

AMTM was formed via a reverse Morris trust (RMT) merger between Amentum and Jacobs’ (ticker J) CMS division in Q3 2024, creating the second-largest public government services provider by revenue. The company operates across five key markets: Defense (46%), Civil (17%), Environmental (15%), Space (11%), and Intelligence (11%), with growing demand across each segment driven by increasing geopolitical tensions, cybersecurity needs, space exploration, and government modernization initiatives. No single contract accounts for more than 4% of total revenue and AMTM’s scale provides a competitive advantage in bidding for larger contracts. About 65% of total revenues are earned through cost-plus contracts, which protects them in a high inflation environment, which would be our base case as Trump aims to reverse a decades long trend of globalization.

The stock has been whipped around like crazy out of the gate on peer’s results and non-stop DOGE headlines but is ignoring its own strong fundamentals after reporting earnings and is cheap on a relative and absolute basis at <10x FCF. With over $500M of annual FCF generation expected, management is focused on reducing the net leverage ratio from the current 4.0x to <3x by the end of FY26, which should lead to multiple expansion (we believe backlog coverage and leverage can explain most of the EBITDA multiple differences among peers).

Jacobs has signaled its intent to sell its remaining 8% stake in the company by the end of June 2025, likely weighing on shares, but this flips to a clear catalyst as that date approaches. Ditto as the two private equity holders whittle their stakes down. As the company integrates operations and executes on synergies from the Jacobs merger, an acceleration of revenue growth would also go a long way towards a multiple re-rating. At a conservative 10.5x 2026E EBITDA, a multiple that will nudge it closer to peers once they have de-levered a bit between now and the end of 2026, the stock has 80% upside to $34.

Conclusion

In the long run, TGH will prove indifferent to every market dogma. In the short run, however, the dogma du jour—such as price momentum—defines him. We reject the rampant fanaticism that often grips the market and fuels investor overconfidence, instead keeping our own fallibility front of mind. Market downturns, inevitable as they are, can either expose hidden speculative vices or serve as a test of our investment virtues—chief among them, valuation discipline.

The macroeconomic news flow at the start of 2025 has been viscous in its density, making it easy to lose sight of an optimal, bottom-up investment approach. A defining feature of the stock market is that it can be made infinitely simple or endlessly complex, depending on one’s perspective. To cut through the noise, we remain focused on identifying idiosyncratic, value- oriented special situations, building a portfolio designed to continue generating uncorrelated alpha amid uncertainty.

Sincerely,

Voss Capital, LP

Appendix:

1 Source: Bank of America Global Research

2 Source: Empirical Research Partners

3 YTD Flows:

  • Cash/Money Market Fund: record January inflows ($174bn)
  • Treasuries: record January inflow ($17bn)
  • Global stocks: biggest January inflow since 2018 ($93bn)
  • US stocks: record January inflow ($76bn)
  • US large cap stocks: record January inflow ($66bn)
  • Emerging Market stocks: 1st January outflow since 2016 ($3bn)

4 Q4 earnings data source is Morgan Stanley.

Common Terms:

CAGR – Compound Annual Growth Rate

IRR – Internal Rate of Return

CAPEX – Capital Expenditures

data.title: CoStar Group: I Still Expect Growth To Accelerate Back To High-Teens
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to CoStar Group (NASDAQ:CSGP) in October 2024, with my key thesis being that the growth runway remains very positive for CSGP. So far, CSGP has continued to demonstrate solid execution, particularly in Apartments.com and Homes.com. Despite ongoing investments weighing on near-term profitability, I believe CSGP’s growth outlook remains very promising; growth should eventually accelerate back to high teens.

Earnings results update

In the latest quarter (4Q24), reported two days ago, CSGP grew revenue by 11% y/y to $709 million, beating management’s guided range of $693-703 million. More notably, adj EBITDA came in at $112 million, up 47% sequentially, showing that the CSGP business can benefit from strong operating leverage despite aggressive investments in Homes.com. Adj EPS came in at $0.26, down ~20% y/y largely because of the higher marketing and sales costs in residential (management had previously signaled this, so it shouldn’t be a surprise). Looking ahead, management guided for $2.985-3.015 billion in revenue, adj EBITDA of $375-405 million, and GAAP net income of $212-233 million.

Apartments.com continues to dominate

Apartments.com revenue grew 13.4% y/y in 4Q24, benefitting from high multifamily vacancy rates and management efforts in optimizing pricing. I wouldn’t be too worried about the growth deceleration sequentially as it was impacted by lower salesforce productivity in 2024 when reps were tasked with selling Homes.com memberships. I think growth should accelerate eventually as sales productivity normalizes and the housing market eventually recovers. This is without a doubt one of CSGP's strongest assets. For reference, Apartments.com generated $1.07 billion in revenue in FY24 (almost 40% of total revenue). What stands out to me is the platform’s ability to sustain premium pricing power while continuing to expand its advertiser base. To put things into perspective (data from the earnings call), Apartments.com delivers 4.8x more leases than Rent.com and Zillow combined. This is strong evidence that Apartments.com is winning share and is expanding its competitive moat in terms of top-of-mind share—which can also be seen from the solid unaided brand awareness figure of 66%.

While management expects Apartments.com revenue to grow only by 11-12% in 2025, I think this is a conservative guide that gives it room to outperform. As you can see from the screenshot above, the total addressable market [TAM] is huge, and CSGP has only captured just 12% of this TAM. It may take a while for CSGP’s salesforce to become efficient in penetrating into the small and mid-sized multifamily market, but given CSGP's track record in winning in the >100 unit range, I think it is very possible that it repeats its success in smaller markets.

Homes.com continues scaling rapidly with Long-Term Potential

Another gem within CSGP’s portfolio is Homes.com, which grew rapidly in 4Q24 by 181% y/y, driving total revenue to $28.1 million (run rate of >$100 million). In my view, Homes.com could potentially become CSGP's biggest growth driver in the coming years. The thing that is remarkable is that although Homes.com remains in investment mode—Salesforce continues to be in expansion mode (management plans to increase dedicated residential sales reps from 277 at the end of 2024 to 500 by year-end 2025)— Homes.com is winning tons of market share. We can see this from the fact that Homes.com overtake Realtor.com with 110 million average monthly unique visitors.

The thing about homes.com is that it is still a small portion of CSGP's business, which I think is a reason why the market did not react as positively as I expected it to after seeing the 181% y/y revenue growth. But remember that Homes.com is still in its early monetization phase, and it has shown how potent growth can be from its 181% y/y revenue growth in 4Q24 (also showing that users are willing to pay for such service). The biggest concern is profitability, as CSGP continues to invest heavily in marketing and sales force expansion. The current guide calls for ~$900 million in residential investment spending in FY25, similar to FY24 levels, with no definitive timeline for breakeven. This may be a concern to some investors, but I reckon management is not irrational. They should have aimed to turn this unit into a profitable segment. At some point, Homes.com’s revenue contribution will become large enough to drive operating leverage, and that could be a catalyst for CSGP valuation multiple to rerate higher.

CRE Market Stabilization

Finally, 2025 should be a good year for CSGP in the commercial real estate market. Management noted that demand for office space turned positive in late 2024 for the first time since 2021, which is very encouraging as it signals a potential turnaround in demand. Other metrics that point to the same direction includes: decline in total available space, decrease in sublet vacancy rates, and sales transactions returning to pre-pandemic levels. This is an important inflection point because CSGP's core CoStar Suite is still a major piece of its business, generating >$1 billion in revenue, which has a high margin. As transaction volumes recover, I would imagine demand for CSGP data and analytics products to improve, and that should drive up overall revenue growth (note that the core CoStar Suite only grew ~5% in 4Q24).

Valuation

I remain positive about CSGP's ability to accelerate growth back to high-teens eventually, driven by a normalized housing market down the road, better sales force efficiency, and continuous monetization of homes.com. Hence, in my model, I believe CSGP can deliver ~$4.4 billion in revenue in FY27, and attaching CSGP's average historical forward revenue multiple of 10.6 would give me a share price target of ~$125. The reason for using CSGP historical multiple is because of my expectation that growth will accelerate back to high-teens, which is higher than the past two years (FY22/23) growth rate of low-teens (despite lower growth rates, FY22/23 traded at an average of 10.6x).

Investment Risk

Depending on how much more CSGP may invest into Homes.com, the timing for profitability to inflect upwards may not happen anytime soon. The situation in the commercial real estate market remains uncertain. If transaction volumes remain poor, CoStar Suite and LoopNet growth could be weaker than expected.

Conclusion

My positive view on CSGP remains unchanged (buy rated) as the company continues executing very well. Both Apartments.com and Homes.com continue to perform well, and I expect growth momentum to continue/improve. The commercial real estate market is also showing early signs of recovery, which is very encouraging. Over the medium to long term, I believe the market will appreciate CSGP’s growth strength and revalue its multiple back to at least its historical average.

data.title: Baron WealthBuilder Fund Q4 2024 Shareholder Letter
data.description:
Dear Baron WealthBuilder Fund Shareholder:

Baron WealthBuilder Fund® (the Fund) performed well in the final quarter of 2024, gaining 5.53% (Institutional Shares). This result was ahead of returns for its mega-cap dominated benchmark, the S&P 500 Index (the Index), and the globally-oriented MSCI ACWI Index (the Global Index), which returned 2.41% and -0.99%, respectively.

In 2024, the Fund gained 18.73%. While this return is respectable in any market environment, it trailed the Index's results although it exceeded the Global Index's performance. Those two indexes returned 25.02% and 17.49%, respectively.

While the Fund modestly trailed the Index since its inception, the Fund's since inception annualized return of 13.62% is ranked in the top percentile of its Morningstar category over that period. While disappointed in the relative returns compared to the Index, we are proud of this long-term ranking achievement in the peer category and absolute results.

As of 12/31/2024, the Morningstar Aggressive Allocation Category consisted of 185, 181, 169, and 176 share classes for the 1-, 3-, 5-year, and since inception (12/29/2017) time periods. Morningstar ranked Baron WealthBuilder Fund Institutional Share Class in the 9th, 93rd, 1st, and 1st percentiles, respectively.

Morningstar calculates the Morningstar Aggressive Allocation Category Average performance and rankings using its Fractional Weighting methodology. Morningstar rankings are based on total returns and do not include sales charges. Total returns do account for management, administrative, and 12b-1 fees and other costs automatically deducted from fund assets.

User is solely responsible for ensuring that any use of this information complies with all laws, regulations and restrictions applicable to it. Neither Morningstar nor its content providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

MORNINGSTAR IS NOT RESPONSIBLE FOR ANY DELETION, DAMAGE, LOSS OR FAILURE TO STORE ANY PRODUCT OUTPUT, COMPANY CONTENT OR OTHER CONTENT.

We believe that after a period of macro-driven pressures, investor confidence is improving. Companies are slowly returning to being valued based on their fundamentals and opportunities dictate the stock price. Inflation has remained manageable. Interest rate cuts should persist. The soft landing the government is attempting to orchestrate is occurring. And the incoming administration appears more accommodating towards growth business objectives.

In the fourth quarter, our more concentrated portfolios performed the best due to substantial gains for their two largest holdings. This strong performance was led by Baron Partners and Focused Growth Funds. Additionally, higher growth and larger-cap portfolios also contributed to returns. This group includes Baron Opportunity and Fifth Avenue Growth Funds. Only a handful of portfolios materially detracted from performance, with the largest being Baron Health Care and Emerging Markets Funds as health care and emerging market equities remained out of favor in the period.

During the fourth quarter, the Fund's two largest underlying holdings, Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) and Space Exploration Technologies Corp. (SPACE), each appreciated more than 50%. Upcoming product launches and improvements in software propelled Tesla shares higher. The latest version of its autonomous driving software showed massive improvements and investors are optimistic that the new presidential administration will more broadly approve its use. This extended deployment would open new and large revenue opportunities for the company. Additionally, upcoming product launches should spur renewed vehicle sales growth in 2025. The Energy business continued to grow, which not only improved top-line revenue but also carried significantly higher margins. The gross profit of the company has improved significantly. More cost reductions should enable a more profitable business in the future.

While these 2 companies were the largest contributors, we had many other underlying holdings across different segments that performed well in the period. While higher growth technology-oriented companies continue to contribute to performance, the next 10 largest contributors included financial services, real estate related, and core growth companies such as Vail Resorts, Inc. (MTN), The Charles Schwab Corporation (SCHW), and Chart Industries, Inc. (GTLS). Many of these companies have proven that prior negative sentiment surrounding their businesses was unfounded. Vail had a resurgence in pass sales and continued to exhibit an ability to increase prices at its resorts. An improvement in sales coupled with a focus on cost management, we believe, should lead to margin expansion in the coming years. Schwab has used strong client inflows to pay down expensive short-term borrowings that should lead to improving net interest margins. And Chart, a leading provider of engineered equipment and solutions to the industrial gas and clean energy industry, demonstrated that demand for its products remains strong. The company showed substantial growth and even greater free cash flow conversion. The company hosted an investor meeting where it described how it can gain market share within the growing industry and expand gross margins over the coming five years.

The largest underlying detractors declined because of near-term, company-specific issues. The CEO at specialty insurer Arch Capital Group Ltd. (ACGL) unexpectedly resigned. He had anticipated stepping down from his position at the end of 2025 but accelerated the timing for a smoother transition and less complexity around decision-making. Investors are awaiting IDEXX Laboratories, Inc.'s (IDXX) new test introductions and smoother clinical visit data. We believe upcoming product introductions will help improve the consistency of its growth rate.

Fund of Funds Structure and Investment Strategy

The Fund is a compilation of our Baron Funds and provides broad equity exposure. All underlying Baron Funds follow a consistent investment philosophy and process. We do not try to mimic the indexes, and we do not alter our strategy to coincide with short-term macro events that we regard as unpredictable. We remain focused on underlying business fundamentals.

We believe small- and mid-cap growth stocks offer attractive return potential relative to their risk over the long term. Small- and mid-cap businesses represent 61.7% of the Fund (compared to only 19.0% for the Index). While our small- and mid-cap growth investments have been successful over our Firm's 42-year history, these styles are occasionally out of favor. The past few years have been one of these environments. Large-cap growth companies outperformed small-cap growth companies during the year and in many instances over the last decade. Since the Fund's inception 7 years ago the 1-year rolling monthly returns of the Russell 1000 Growth Index have outperformed the Russell 2000 Growth Index 78% of the time including 6 out of the past 7 calendar years.

Rather than only examining the Fund's performance over a quarter or a year, we believe it is equally important to understand how the Fund has performed over the course of an economic cycle. The COVID pandemic and subsequent Macro-Induced Market Rotation has been very difficult for small- and mid-sized growth companies. Investors have favored large-cap, value-oriented businesses that are deemed safer during a time of uncertainty. We believe this offers a great opportunity for long-term investors to invest in small- and mid-cap growth businesses at attractive prices. Markets first peaked in late February 2020 before rapidly dropping as the economy braced for the COVID pandemic. It recovered quickly before experiencing another sizable drop based on macroeconomic factors. Over the three years of the COVID pandemic ended 12/31/2022, the Russell 2000 Growth Index, a small-cap growth index, gained only 1.96% on a cumulative basis. The Russell Midcap Growth Index fared better with a cumulative 3-year return of 12.00%. With that backdrop, the Fund performed better and appreciated 28.11%. We believe protecting and growing clients' assets during this challenging period positions long-term investors well for meaningful appreciation once the macro landscape changes. Table III provides a more complete look at how the Fund and various indexes performed during the pandemic and its aftermath.

We do not yet know if the challenges caused by the COVID pandemic will persist. Volatility has remained high, but we are hopeful that interest rate increases, policy factors, and COVID hangovers are ending. Global conflict has increased and geopolitics remains uncertain. But growth companies have recently begun to outperform value. So far, we are optimistic that companies will again be valued on their fundamentals rather than on macroeconomic concerns. We have started to see the performance of some individual securities diverge. This divergence of returns, we believe, should favor growth investors. Given our weightings, the Fund's performance has trailed the large-cap Index since the start of this cycle. However, the Fund's return has continued to meaningfully exceed small-cap growth indexes.

While we present the Fund's absolute and relative returns over the SEC mandated periods, we believe it is also important to discuss how the Fund performs over the course of different market environments. The three-year period ended 12/31/2022 was a difficult time for growth investors. It was a period punctuated by a global pandemic, geopolitical instability, and macroeconomic headwinds. The VIX was trading at above-average levels, while Barra factor returns for Beta and Growth were low. Despite these challenges, the Fund's high-quality growth portfolio weathered the period well, marginally exceeding the Index. The Fund returned 8.61% annualized compared to the Index's return of 7.66%.

However, the economy and markets have transitioned, in our opinion, over the prior two years. This market transition to a more constructive environment has been (temporarily) challenging. This started at the end of 2022 and extended into 2024. The Fund's underlying holdings are a balanced group of distinct types of quality-growth businesses. Customers at many service businesses had retreated causing revenue growth to moderate. Suppliers had increased prices causing margins to be pressured. Higher interest rates increased financing costs and raised the discount on future earnings. Investors gravitated towards more speculative, lower-quality businesses, and mega-cap companies with AI opportunities. These businesses do not have material representation in the underlying portfolios, which has resulted in an annualized return of 22.18% for the Fund since 12/31/2022. While substantial on an absolute basis, this return trailed the Index's annualized gain of 25.65%

This transitional period appears to be ending. Over the prior two quarters, the Fund had a cumulative return of 16.26% compared to 8.44% for the Index. We are optimistic that a sustained constructive market will result in both good absolute and relative returns for the Funds, similar to how most Baron Funds have performed since their inceptions.

data.title: Comcast: Why I Disagree With Wall Street
data.description:
CMCSA stock: Wall Street now loves it

I presented a bullish thesis on Comcast Corporation (NASDAQ:CMCSA) in my last analysis as you can see from the following screenshot. To wit, my last Seeking Alpha article on the stock is titled "Comcast Stock Fits The 10x EBT Rule Nicely (Rating Upgrade)" and was published on April 24, 2024. The sentiment surrounding the stock was quite negative and as a contrarian investor, I rated the stock as "Strong Buy" based on the following considerations:

Comcast Corporation stock has delivered a robust return since my last BUY thesis in late 2022. The stock is now even stronger BUY in my view. Trading at 7x pretax earnings, it is a strong fit for Warren Buffett’s 10x EBT Rule.

At the time of publication, CMCSA's price was $38.65. The stock indeed enjoyed a strong rally shortly afterwards and reached a 52-week high of over $45 before it suffered a severe correction recently.

Besides the price fluctuations, there have been a few other new catalysts surrounding CMCSA. In the remainder of this article, I will focus on the top 2 on my list: the sentiment shift and also its recent Q4 earnings report. I will see why I disagree with Wall Street’s prevailing bullish sentiment surrounding the stock. As you can see from the next chart below, Wall Street loves CMCSA now, in the last 90 days, 50% of Wall Street analysts (15 out of 30) gave the stock a "Buy" or "Strong Buy" ratings, and 50% of them (15 out of 30) recommended a "Hold", with no analysts suggesting a "Sell".

Next, I will review the updates the company provided in its recent Q4 ER and explain I think Wall Street is overstating its growth outlook based on these updates. This reexamination of the stock also promoted me to adjust my rating on the stock to HOLD under current conditions.

CMCSA stock: Q4 earnings and growth outlook

Admittedly, CMCSA demonstrated strong performance in the past quarter (with earnings announced on January 30, 2025), providing a good reason for Wall Street to feel bullish. The company beat consensus estimates on both lines with good margins. To wit, it reported a normalized EPS of $0.96, surpassing estimates by $0.10 (i.e., more than 10%). GAAP EPS was even stronger at $1.24, beating estimates by an even larger margin of $0.48. Revenue also exceeded expectations, reaching $31.92B and beating estimates by $291.58M.

Looking ahead, consensus EPS estimates for CMCSA point to continued growth at a healthy pace. For FY 2025, the EPS estimate is $4.28, reflecting a slight speedbump and a YOY decline of 1.26%. However, growth is expected to resume afterward, with EPS estimates of $4.75 for FY 2026 (10.99% YOY growth) and $5.09 for 2027 (7.30% growth). All told, a 6.6% compound annual growth rate (CAGR) is projected over the next 5 years.

Based on these EPS estimates, CMCSA's forward P/E ratios appear highly attractive. The ratio is estimated at 8.53 for FY1 (i.e., based on 2025’s EPS estimate). The ratio is then expected to decrease quickly to 7.69 in 2026, 7.16 in 2027, and only 6.96 in 2028.

But I think these projections are too optimistic as detailed next.

CMCSA stock: growth is overestimated

My method for estimating long-term growth for mature companies like CMCSA involves the use of ROCE (return on capital employed) and reinvestment rates. The details are provided in an earlier article for readers interested. Here, I will just quote the end results:

For businesses like CMCSA, I consider the following items capital actually employed 1) Working capital, including payables, receivables, inventory, 2) Gross Property, Plant, and Equipment, and 3) Research and development expenses (a relatively minor expense for CMCSA) are also amortized and considered part of its capital employed. Under these considerations, CMCSA’s ROCE has been averaging 49% in recent years as seen in the next chart.

As for its reinvestment rate (RR), my estimate is about 8% to 10% based on its CAPEX spending in recent years. The company spends heavily on CAPEX (about 45% of its operating cash in recent years). However, as a capital-intensive business, a dominant portion of the CAPEX is for maintenance, and the growth RR is about 8% to 10% by my estimate. With a ROCE of 49% and a RR of 8~10%, the organic growth rate is about 4~5% in my model (8% RR * 49% ROCE = 3.9% growth rate), significantly below the consensus projection mentioned on the foregoing discussion.

Other risks and final thoughts

The above discrepancy in its growth rate would fundamentally change the valuation assessment of the stock too. I feel an article on a telecom stock would be incomplete without discussing the dividend yields. (CMCSA is currently yielding 3.6% at the price of this writing). As such, I will use the so-called PEGY promoted by Peter Lynch to illustrate how the growth rate projection impact the valuation assessment.

For dividend stocks, Lynch uses a revised version of the PEG ratio - the PEGY ratio, which is defined as the P/E ratio divided by the sum of the earnings growth rate and dividend yield. The idea behind the PEGY is very simple and effective (most effective ideas are simple). If a stock pays out a large part of its earnings as dividends, then investors do not need a high growth rate to enjoy healthy returns. And vice versa. And similar to the PEG ratio, his preference is a PEGY ratio of 1x or below.

For CMCSA, with an FWD P/E of 8.53 and a yield of 3.6%+, the PEGY ratio would be only 0.84x if the 6.6% CAGR projected by consensus materializes. It is substantially below the ideal threshold of 1x and leaves a wide margin of safety. However, with my projected 4~5% growth rate, the PEGY ratio is between 1 and 1.12, leaving little or no margin of safety. Also note my above estimate is for its organic growth rate. Comcast has plans in motion to spin off some of its segments (such as MSNBC and other pay-TV networks). These spinoffs, of course, will need to go through all the approval steps (approval from the board of directors, certain regulators, etc.). My estimate is that these spinoffs should take place sometime toward the end of 2025. The impacts of spinoffs could further reduce the growth potential of CMCSA.

To recap, the follow-up analysis on CMCSA is motivated by the sentiment shift and also its recent Q4 ER. On the surface, the company reported a strong quarter, providing a good reason for the prevailing bullish sentiment. However, my assessment is that the market overstates its growth outlook, which in turn also exaggerates the attractiveness of its valuation. For these reasons, I disagree with Wall Street’s buy rating and rate the stock as a HOLD under current conditions.

data.title: 2 Market Themes For Investors To Consider
data.description:
While trade policy has been a focus for many investors, Justin Flowerday, Managing Director and Head of Public Equities, TD Asset Management, says there are two other themes investors may want on their radar. He discusses with MoneyTalk’s Greg Bonnell.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Well, there are no shortage of risks out there for investors. US markets continue to hit record highs. But are corporate earnings justifying these valuations? Joining us now to discuss, Justin Flowerday, Managing Director, Head of Public Equities at TD Asset Management.

Justin, great to have you back on the program.

Justin Flowerday: Thanks for having me back, Greg.

Greg Bonnell: So it's interesting. We do see-- if you pick up any newspaper, you watch any newscast-- or I guess young people don't do either of those things. They get their information somehow. But the headlines can-- they can be overwhelming. Yet, the markets continue to power to new highs. And we've got a lot of earnings behind us. What are you seeing out there?

Justin Flowerday: Yeah, so look, we're entering the tail end of Q4 earnings season. And the numbers have been solid. It's been a solid kind of set of results. You've seen 12%-ish, 13% earnings growth on the back of 5% revenue growth, so nice bottom line growth driven by decent top line growth with some margin expansion. And I'd say the really positive thing that's coming out of this earnings season is the fact that it is broadening out. It's not just big tech. It's not just Mag Seven. We're seeing participation from other sectors.

If we were talking a year ago and you asked me how is everything going, I would have said, look, it's a tale of two worlds, tale of two cities. You have big tech, Mag Seven generating 30%, 40% earnings growth, the rest of the market kind of 2% to 3%. We're now seeing bigger contributions from financials, industrials, utilities, healthcare. And so, that's been a bit of a positive coming out of this earnings season.

The only other thing I'd mention is just we obviously listen to a lot of conference calls. And I would say there is a tone of optimism that has emerged from management teams on conference calls this earnings season that we haven't seen in some time. They're just very confident about the demand backdrop. They're confident about this wave of inventory restocking that's taking place. They're confident about their ability to take costs out of their infrastructure and automate certain processes over the coming years.

So look, they're not-- you mentioned HudBay (HBM, HBM:CA) guidance being negative earlier today. Management teams are generally not super-optimistic with their guidance. So, they're not saying 2025 is going to be amazing. But their tone is optimistic.

Greg Bonnell: Some of the major themes, I think, playing out against the backdrop of all that-- I know you've been keeping an eye on it-- and one of them is reindustrialization of the Western world. If we're going to start bringing production back here, what does that mean in the longer run, not only for the economy but for some of these corporations?

Justin Flowerday: Sure. Yeah. And look, this isn't a new story. But I think it is something to pay attention to because it's gaining momentum. And the reality is, we have had an underinvestment in infrastructure in the Western world for the last several decades. And it's really starting to play havoc in the economy and create frictions in the economy.

And look, lots of areas to go here-- transportation, for example, is one area that we often speak about. Whether it's aviation or trains or driving, or just taking the subway, we have an underinvestment in the transportation networks and a deterioration in the transportation networks across North America. You and I live in Toronto, and we see the frustrations here. It's not just Toronto.

Greg Bonnell: I have had some difficulties in recent days getting to work and home from work, a little bit of difficulty.

Justin Flowerday: Even without the snow.

Greg Bonnell: Even without the snow.

Justin Flowerday: Even without the snow, there's difficulties. And so, look, this is just-- again, it's a friction for the consumer. It's a friction for businesses. And there's been, I think, an understanding and a responsiveness from governments and private companies who are saying, look, we need to do something about this, and we need to start reinvesting. And it probably started with the pandemic five years ago-- I can't believe that's five years ago-- when we saw the supply chain inefficiencies. Geopolitical risks have now shown our vulnerability. And so, our need for resilient domestic manufacturing and resilient domestic infrastructure is certainly at play here.

Greg Bonnell: So that's the reindustrialization of the Western world theme. Also, I think a lot of people headed into this year thinking that we'd see some deregulation of certain industries that would help their bottom lines.

Justin Flowerday: Sure. Yeah. No, and this is-- look, we've had a lot of talk about tariffs. And we've had a lot of talk about trade wars. And I think one of the themes that hasn't been discussed very much has been this theme of deregulation that's taking place in North America and in Europe and in Asia. And if you think about it, the US, we're hearing headlines every single day-- we've heard more today-- about just general deregulation of the financial system. We're going to see less oversight, let's just call it, of certain sections of the banks in the US. Capital adequacy will be probably a little bit more lenient. That's going to be a positive.

But if you go to Europe, there's deregulation taking there. It's taking place in the energy sector. We've had government oversight of energy prices for quite some time. And I think there's now a real movement which is leading towards more market-driven energy prices.

And the last region that-- I think this is actually the poster child for deregulation, is Japan, where you've seen-- and it's been talked about a bunch-- but you're still seeing it, deregulation, in terms of pension reform, in terms of labor practices, hiring and firing, in terms of the energy system and the financial system. So, lots of really positive themes there.

The positive here is that, again it goes back to frictions and removing frictions from companies and consumers. And this just ultimately-- if you look historically, deregulation ultimately leads to higher levels of profitability for corporations, which supports earnings growth for the medium term.

Greg Bonnell: We put those big themes together. And obviously-- I feel like we're living in this world where a constant amount of information coming at us, coming at us. We don't know what to make of it. But we're speaking about longer-term themes here. When an investor thinks about these things, how should they be thinking about investing?

Justin Flowerday: Well, the way we think about investing is trying to understand where some of these demand tailwinds are going to be coming from and allocating capital into industries that will, first of all, have a benefit from long-term demand tailwinds. And then once you've done that, really, it's about finding the right management team and the right company that's going to be able to capture the greatest portion of free cash flow from a certain industry. And if you do that, and you try not to get too cute, you can be quite successful over the long term.
